task_completion.description: Open the initial boundary_plat.dxf file in the CAD software.
task_completion.visual_evidence: The CAD software (e.g., TopoCal) is open and the file 'boundary_plat.dxf' is loaded, displaying a four-sided property boundary polyline on the canvas.

task_completion.description: Add an aligned linear dimension to one side of the polyline.
task_completion.visual_evidence: An aligned linear dimension is visible along at least one side of the polyline, with extension lines accurately snapped to the corner vertices.

task_completion.description: Add aligned linear dimensions to all four sides of the polyline.
task_completion.visual_evidence: Aligned linear dimensions are clearly visible on all four sides of the property boundary polyline, accurately placed at the vertices.

A 'Save' or 'Save As' dialog box shows the file being saved as 'boundary_dimensioned.top' in the C:\Users\Docker\Documents\TopoCal_Project\ directory, or the application title bar updates to reflect this new filename.
An 'Export' or 'Save As' dialog box shows the drawing being saved/exported as 'boundary_dimensioned.dxf' in the C:\Users\Docker\Documents\TopoCal_Project\ directory.

integrity.description: The agent used the CAD software's graphical user interface to add dimensions.
integrity.visual_evidence: Screenshots show the agent clicking on dimensioning tools in the toolbar/ribbon and interacting with the drawing canvas, rather than using a terminal or script to modify the file.

integrity.description: The agent used the software's native menus to save and export the files.
integrity.visual_evidence: Screenshots show interaction with 'File > Save', 'File > Save As', or 'File > Export' menus and standard OS file dialogs, not terminal commands.

integrity.description: The agent did not manually edit the DXF file using a text editor.
integrity.visual_evidence: The DXF file is never seen opened in Notepad, VS Code, or any other text editor to manually inject dimension entities.
